storm.summary_layperson: Skies are clear and no storms are in the area this evening — the only unsettled weather is a cold front expected tomorrow night into Monday.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Long Island Sound water is 72°F, but there are no nearby storms for it to weaken or redirect tonight.
storm.detail_html:
The regional picture is calm. No NWS watches, warnings, or advisories are in effect. KOKX radar shows no storm cells within range — reflectivity returns are absent and the storm-arrival engine has no inbound targets to track. CAPE is 0 J/kg, confirming a stable air mass with no convective energy overhead.

Bulk wind shear is elevated at 37.8 kt (0–6 km, WSW). In a high-CAPE environment this would be a serious severe-weather ingredient. With CAPE at zero, there is nothing to organize — shear is irrelevant to tonight's conditions. The KOKX Area Forecast Discussion references an approaching frontal system bringing showers and thunderstorms, but places that activity on Monday, not tonight.

Long Island Sound water is 72°F, sitting 3–4°F below the air. This creates a weak marine stable layer that can moderate or deflect incoming convection — but with no approaching cells, this mechanism plays no role this evening.

Nothing in the current data changes this assessment. The only scenario that would raise risk is a new cell firing ahead of tomorrow's front and moving toward the coast — not supported by CAPE=0 and absent upstream initiation on radar.

wind.regime_label: Sea-breeze day — late afternoon, thermal collapsing at sunset (20:20)

hourly.shift_note: sea breeze fading, direction drifting left
thermal collapsing near sunset, direction variable

wind.tactics_blurb: Pure southerly sea breeze — the cleanest racing direction for the cove. The first race (17:00–18:00) is the best window at 6–7 kt; breeze softens steadily toward sunset at 20:20 and will die below 3 kt around 20:00. Work the center of the course and play small ±10° oscillations. Skip the last race.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
This is a sea-breeze day. Inland temperatures are running 78–80°F (ensemble median 78.4°F) while Long Island Sound water is 71.6°F — an air-to-water gap of roughly 6–8°F, enough to sustain a thermal sea breeze through early evening. The background gradient is light and offshore (NW 7 kt at Westchester), well below the threshold that would suppress the thermal. The wind at the cove tonight is driven almost entirely by the sea breeze, not the synoptic pressure pattern.

The sea breeze is fully established right now. RYC reports S 175° at 6.5 kt with a 10-minute average of 6.6 kt, gusting to 10.6 kt. Coastal confirmation across Long Island Sound: KGON (Groton) S 9 kt, KHVN (New Haven) SSW 6 kt, KWST (Westerly) SW 10 kt. The breeze is mature and covers the full CT coast. The HRRR model (19Z cycle) already shows NW gradient wind from 17:00 EDT onward — roughly 1–2 hours early. This is a known model lag: HRRR tends to exit the sea-breeze regime before observations do. For the 17:00–19:00 period, the observation-based sea-breeze direction (S/SSW/SW) overrides the model's NW call.

Sunset is at 20:20 EDT. Under a pure sea-breeze regime, the thermal driver begins collapsing 30–60 minutes before sunset as land-surface heating shuts off. Expect the breeze to soften noticeably from 19:00 EDT onward and drop below 3 kt around 20:00, near sunset. After sunset, a very light NNE gradient (~3–4 kt) fills in per the HRRR post-collapse state (fh=5–6 showing NNE 11–18°, 3.5–3.9 kt).

The cove runs due north–south and opens due south, making a pure southerly sea breeze the cleanest possible racing direction — straight up the axis with no shelter asymmetry. Pressure is relatively even across the starting line. With the mature SSW component, there is a slight advantage to the center and east side (the west shore sits in fractional lee of Belle Haven on westerly components). In today's S/SSW, this difference is small — play the oscillations rather than committing hard to one side. The mature sea breeze oscillates ±8–12° around its mean on 10–15 minute cycles.

Timing is the key tactical call tonight. The first race (starting ~17:00–17:30) is the best race: 6–7 kt, steady S/SSW, mature oscillating sea breeze. By 18:30 the breeze is softening into the 5–6 kt range. By 19:00 EDT — 1 hour 20 minutes before sunset — it drops to 4 kt and begins losing reliability. Any race starting after 19:00 EDT is at serious risk of finishing in dying or variable air below 3 kt. Race committees should plan accordingly.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (WLIS_MET) observation is 75 minutes old — over-water wind and SST may not reflect current conditions.

HRRR (19Z cycle) shows NW gradient from 17:00 EDT; observed sea breeze (RYC S 6.5 kt, KGON S 9 kt) is ~1–2 hours ahead of the model — hourly forecast for 17:00–19:00 EDT uses observation-corrected directions.
